questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Parts of the following sentence have been given as options. Select the option that contains a grammatical error. Bobo was / very richer than Jim / as he had illicitly accumulated / all his wealth.
options: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} as he had illicitly accumulated
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The option that contains a grammatical error is: very richer than Jim The correct comparative form of the adjective "rich" is "richer." Therefore, the correct phrase should be "richer than Jim" instead of "very richer than Jim." Therefore, the corrected answer would be: “Bobo was richer than Jim as he had illicitly accumulated all his wealth.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate option to replace the underlined segment in the given sentence. They s̲p̲o̲k̲e̲ about the political turmoil in the state.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to replace the underlined segment in the given sentence is: talked The word "talked" is a suitable synonym for "spoke" in this context, as it maintains the meaning of discussing or conversing about something. Stated: Expressed or declared something explicitly or formally. Expressed: Conveyed or communicated thoughts, feelings, or ideas, often through words, actions, or other forms of communication.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate ANTONYM of the given word. Accept
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate antonym of the given word "Accept" is: Deny "Accept" means to agree to receive or undertake something that is offered or suggested. "Deny," means to refuse to admit the truth or existence of something or to refuse to grant or allow something. Confirm: To establish the truth, correctness, or validity of something. Respond: To reply or react to something, typically in words or actions. Agree: To have the same opinion or hold the same view as someone else.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the option that is similar in meaning to the underlined word. He gave a c̲o̲n̲c̲i̲s̲e̲ explanation of the company’s retirement plan.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is c: Terse "Concise" means giving a lot of information clearly and in a few words. "Terse" is an adjective that describes something brief and to the point, often to the extent of being curt or abrupt. Verbose: Using or expressed in more words than are needed. Clear: Easy to perceive, understand, or interpret; not obscured or confused. Sharp: Having an edge or point that is able to cut or pierce something.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Sentences of a paragraph are given below in jumbled order. Arrange the sentences in the correct order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph. A.The principal wished the students continued to aim for perfection in their future endeavours. B.The principal also lauded educators for their tireless efforts to educate students and inspire them to come up with new ideas. C.Our college's principal delivered an excellent speech in which he lauded the pupils. D.The principal mentioned how teaching assistants assisted students in resolving academic and social challenges.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph is: CDBA Sentence C introduces the main topic of the paragraph, which is the speech delivered by the college's principal. Sentence D elaborates on the principal's mention of teaching assistants and their role in assisting students. Sentence B follows, discussing how the principal praised educators for their efforts in educating and inspiring students. Sentence A concludes the paragraph by mentioning the principal's wishes for the students' future endeavors.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate ANTONYM of the word vivid from the given sentence: In my murky memory lane, I still could vividly apprehend and envision the pictures of Nischindipu while reading Bibhutibhushan Bandyopadhyay’s “Pather Panchali”
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate antonym of the word "vivid" in the given sentence is: murky "Vivid" means clear, bright, or distinct, especially in one's memory or imagination. Envision: To imagine or visualize something in one's mind; to picture or foresee something as a future possibility or outcome. Perceive: To become aware of or recognize through the senses, especially sight, hearing, or touch. Murky: Dark, gloomy, or obscure; lacking in clarity or transparency; characterized by dimness or cloudiness, especially in water. Apprehend: To understand or perceive something, typically with difficulty or effort; to grasp or comprehend mentally.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The following sentence has been split into four segments. Identify the segment that contains a grammatical error. They all, except Shirin, were very sad / during the farewell party given / to the teacher retiring, and her / indifference were well noticed.
options: They all, except Shirin, were very sad
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} during the farewell party given
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} to the teacher retiring, and her
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is b: indifference were well noticed The subject "indifference" is singular, so it should be followed by the singular verb "was" instead of the plural verb "were." Therefore, the corrected segment is "indifference was well noticed." Therefore, the corrected answer would be: “They all, except Shirin, were very sad during the farewell party given to the teacher retiring, and her indifference was well noticed.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the correct active form of the given sentence. Luckily, the machinery was not used by them.
options: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} Luckily, they have not used the machinery.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Luckily, they did not use the machinery.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Luckily, they had not used the machinery.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Luckily, I did not used the machinery.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is b: Luckily, they did not use the machinery. Active voice ---- Passive voice (Simple past) Subject + do/did + not + V1 + Object ---- Object + was/were + V3 + by + Subject Therefore, the corrected answer would be: “Luckily, they did not use the machinery.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate option that can substitute the underlined segment in the given sentence. Unemployment has b̲e̲c̲o̲m̲e̲ ̲a̲ ̲f̲i̲e̲r̲y̲ ̲q̲u̲e̲s̲t̲i̲o̲n̲ of the day.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option that can substitute the underlined segment "a fiery question" in the given sentence is: become a burning question The phrase "a burning question" is a common idiom used to describe an urgent or pressing issue that demands immediate attention. It conveys the idea of something that is highly significant or contentious.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the correct meaning of the given idiom. Turn over a new leaf
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct meaning of the idiom "Turn over a new leaf" is: To begin again The idiom "Turn over a new leaf" means to start anew, to make a fresh start, or to change one's behavior or attitude for the better. It implies leaving behind old habits, mistakes, or ways of living and adopting a new and improved way of life.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Parts of the following sentence have been given as options. Select the option that contains a grammatical error. If you don’t find any error, mark ‘No error’ as your answer. Her concerts / are very admired / by the foreigners / no error.
options: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} by the foreigners.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} are very admired
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The part that contains a grammatical error is: are very admired n the given sentence, "very" is not necessary before "admired." The verb "admired" can stand alone without an intensifier like "very" or "greatly." Therefore, the grammatically correct sentence would be: "Her concerts are admired by the foreigners."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the correct active form of the given sentence. You are asked to tell the plumber to mend all the pipes.
options: All the pipes must be mend.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Tell the plumber mending all the pipes.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Tell the plumber to mend all the pipes.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Command the plumber mending all the pipes.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct active form of the given sentence is: "Tell the plumber to mend all the pipes." In the active voice, the subject performs the action expressed by the verb. Therefore, "Tell the plumber to mend all the pipes" is the correct active form of the given sentence. It correctly conveys the instruction for someone (in this case, "you") to inform the plumber about the task of repairing all the pipes.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank. I am ___________ after five years.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank is: “taking an exam” The phrase "taking an exam" is commonly used to indicate the action of participating in an examination. Therefore, in the context of the sentence, "taking an exam" best fits the meaning intended, suggesting that the speaker will be sitting for an exam after five years.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the option that can be used as a one-word substitute for the given group of words. Holding on to something or keeping an opinion with determination
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is c: Tenacious "Tenacious" means holding firmly to something, whether it's an idea, opinion, or physical object, with determination and persistence. Obnoxious: Extremely unpleasant, offensive, or repulsive; causing strong dislike or annoyance. Loquacious: Tending to talk a great deal; talkative or chatty; fond of or given to continuous conversation. Gracious: Courteous, kind, and pleasant, especially towards others; marked by kindness, tact, and generosity.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate meaning of the underlined idiom in the given sentence. A̲ ̲f̲a̲i̲r̲-̲w̲e̲a̲t̲h̲e̲r̲ ̲f̲r̲i̲e̲n̲d̲ can never be trusted in the times of exigency.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is c: A selfish friend A "fair-weather friend" is someone who is friendly and supportive only when things are going well but abandons or betrays you in times of trouble or difficulty. They are not reliable or trustworthy during times of need.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the INCORRECTLY spelt word.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is a: Sinhaleese The correct spelling is: Sinhalese Sinhalese: Referring to the people of Sri Lanka, or the language spoken by the majority ethnic group of Sri Lanka, the Sinhalese people. Conform: To comply with rules, standards, or expectations; to behave according to socially accepted conventions or norms. Confluence: The junction or meeting point where two or more streams, rivers, or other bodies of water come together and flow as one. Sabbatical: A period of leave granted to an employee (often a professor or researcher) for study, travel, or rest, usually taken every seven years.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the word segment that substitutes (replaces) the bracketed word segment correctly and completes the sentence meaningfully: Estella had hardly heard the news (after) she began to weep.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is c: when "When" is used to indicate the time at which something happened. In this context, it indicates that Estella began to weep immediately after hearing the news.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Parts of the given sentence have been given as options. One of them contains a spelling error. Select the option that rectifies the error. In research done by an online publecation it was found that the most popular password is 123456!
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is d: Publication The correct spelling of "publecation" is "publication."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank and complete the collocation. Happiness can be spread by being grateful or ________ compliments to someone.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank and complete the collocation is: paying Sharing: To divide and distribute something among a group of people; to have a portion of something with others. Accepting: To receive or take something offered; to agree to something. Paying: To give money for goods or services. Giving: To freely transfer the possession of something to someone; to bestow or grant.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate ANTONYM of the word ‘PENNILESS’ from the given sentence. Living in a house with a spacious lawn a proper parking space and a nice swimming pool is still a dream for many rich people in Mumbai.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is b: Rich Penniless: Having no money; very poor. Space: An area that is free or unoccupied; the physical extent of an area. Rich: Having a great deal of money or assets; wealthy. Dream: A series of thoughts, images, and sensations occurring in a person's mind during sleep. Spacious: Having ample space; large and roomy.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank. The thing nodded vigorously; as it nodded, the left button-eye fell off and (1) ______ (2) ______ the concrete floor. The thing looked around (3) ______ with its one eye, as if it had lost her. The thing twisted bonelessly (4) ______ its one eye was again facing her. And it (5) ______ across the cellar towards her then. Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 1.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in blank number 1 is: Clattered Clattered: Made a continuous rattling sound as of hard objects falling or striking each other. Hissed: Made a sharp sibilant sound, similar to the sound made by a snake. Buzzed: Made a low, continuous humming sound. Fizzed: Made a hissing or bubbling sound, typically because of the release of gas.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank. The thing nodded vigorously; as it nodded, the left button-eye fell off and (1) ______ (2) ______ the concrete floor. The thing looked around (3) ______ with its one eye, as if it had lost her. The thing twisted bonelessly (4) ______ its one eye was again facing her. And it (5) ______ across the cellar towards her then. Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 2.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in blank number 2 is: onto "onto" correctly describes the motion of the button-eye as it falls and lands on the concrete floor. To: Expressing motion in the direction of (a particular location). At: Expressing location or arrival in a particular place or position. Up: From a lower to a higher point on (something); along.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank. The thing nodded vigorously; as it nodded, the left button-eye fell off and (1) ______ (2) ______ the concrete floor. The thing looked around (3) ______ with its one eye, as if it had lost her. The thing twisted bonelessly (4) ______ its one eye was again facing her. And it (5) ______ across the cellar towards her then. Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 3.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in blank number 3 is: vacantly Vacantly: In a manner showing lack of thought or intelligence; emptily. Wittily: In a clever or amusing manner. Involuntarily: Without will or conscious control; automatically. Ferociously: In a fierce, savage, or violent manner.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank. The thing nodded vigorously; as it nodded, the left button-eye fell off and (1) ______ (2) ______ the concrete floor. The thing looked around (3) ______ with its one eye, as if it had lost her. The thing twisted bonelessly (4) ______ its one eye was again facing her. And it (5) ______ across the cellar towards her then. Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 4.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in blank number 4 is: until Here, "until" indicates the duration of time during which the thing twisted bonelessly until its one eye was again facing her. Unless: Except if (used to introduce a case in which a statement being made is not true or valid). Even so: Despite that; nevertheless. Ever after: Happily, forever after; for the rest of one's life.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank. The thing nodded vigorously; as it nodded, the left button-eye fell off and (1) ______ (2) ______ the concrete floor. The thing looked around (3) ______ with its one eye, as if it had lost her. The thing twisted bonelessly (4) ______ its one eye was again facing her. And it (5) ______ across the cellar towards her then. Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 5.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in blank number 5 is: Lunged Lunged: Made a sudden forward movement, typically to attack or reach something. Rung: The sound made by a bell when struck; a level in a hierarchy or organization. Flung: Threw something forcefully and with a sudden movement. Hung: Suspended or attached from above with the lower part not supported.
